name: Nazca Hummingbird
description: The Nazca Hummingbird is one of the most recognizable geoglyphs in the Nazca Lines, a UNESCO World Heritage Site in southern Peru. Mainstream archaeology attributes the Nazca Lines, including the Hummingbird, to the Nazca culture, who created them between 500 BCE and 500 CE. They are believed to have served ritualistic or astronomical purposes, possibly related to water and fertility. Proponents of alternative theories suggest the lines were created by ancient astronauts, a lost civilization with advanced technology, or for purposes beyond simple ritual, such as landing strips for extraterrestrial vehicles or maps for celestial navigation. These theories often rely on the precision and scale of the geoglyphs, arguing that such feats would have been impossible for the Nazca people with their presumed level of technology. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, citing evidence of surveying techniques and social organization within the Nazca culture that could have enabled the creation of the lines. Experimental archaeology has demonstrated how the lines could have been created using simple tools and mathematical knowledge available to the Nazca people. The Hummingbird, in particular, is often cited as evidence of advanced planning and execution, but archaeological studies suggest that its creation was within the capabilities of the Nazca civilization.
subtitle: Giant desert drawing of a bird.

claims.claim: The Nazca Lines, including the Hummingbird, were created by ancient astronauts.
claims.evidence: The precision and scale of the lines suggest advanced technology beyond the capabilities of the Nazca people.
claims.criticism: Archaeological evidence supports the Nazca people's ability to create the lines using simple tools and surveying techniques. No credible evidence supports extraterrestrial involvement.

claims.claim: The Hummingbird and other Nazca Lines served as landing strips for alien spacecraft.
claims.evidence: The straight lines and large scale resemble runways.
claims.criticism: The lines are uneven and unsuitable for aircraft landing. Their orientation and location do not align with modern runway designs.

claims.claim: The geoglyphs are part of a larger, undiscovered system of communication or energy grid.
claims.evidence: The precise geometric shapes and connections between different geoglyphs suggest a deeper underlying purpose.
claims.criticism: There is no physical evidence of a larger system or energy grid. The geometric shapes can be explained by ritualistic and astronomical alignments significant to the Nazca culture.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the ancient astronaut theory.

keyFigures.name: Maria Reiche
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who dedicated her life to studying and preserving the Nazca Lines.

keyFigures.name: Anthony Aveni
keyFigures.role: Archaeoastronomer who studied the astronomical alignments of the Nazca Lines.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to Andean bird deities and shamanic practices.
Symbolism related to water and fertility.

timeline.event: Period of Nazca culture activity and creation of the Nazca Lines.
Nazca Lines rediscovered by modern aviation.
Erich von Däniken popularizes the ancient astronaut theory, including claims about the Nazca Lines.
Ongoing archaeological research and debate about the purpose and creation of the Nazca Lines.
